title: Fix ignored --stop-timeout flag to 'podman create'
body: Also add some extra debug information to help figure out what's going on when stop goes bad.

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Hey, so, in testing (rootless), I'm seeing:
```
./bin/podman --log-level=debug stop --timeout=5 foo
INFO[0000] running as rootless                          
DEBU[0000] Initializing boltdb state at /home/esm/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db 
DEBU[0000] Using graph driver overlay                   
DEBU[0000] Using graph root /home/esm/.local/share/containers/storage 
DEBU[0000] Using run root /run/user/14904               
DEBU[0000] Using static dir /home/esm/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod 
DEBU[0000] Using tmp dir /run/user/14904/libpod/tmp     
DEBU[0000] Using volume path /home/esm/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes 
DEBU[0000] Set libpod namespace to ""                   
DEBU[0000] Not configuring container store              
DEBU[0000] Setting maximum workers to 24                
DEBU[0000] Stopping ctr 76bd77687b93a1c15c2c790e920b6bd882a90ca40920d60dde6ff693dd7b5bfc with timeout 5 
DEBU[0000] Stopping container 76bd77687b93a1c15c2c790e920b6bd882a90ca40920d60dde6ff693dd7b5bfc (PID 24516) 
DEBU[0000] Sending signal 15 to container 76bd77687b93a1c15c2c790e920b6bd882a90ca40920d60dde6ff693dd7b5bfc 
WARN[0005] Timed out stopping container 76bd77687b93a1c15c2c790e920b6bd882a90ca40920d60dde6ff693dd7b5bfc, resorting to SIGKILL 
ERRO[0008] Error saving container 76bd77687b93a1c15c2c790e920b6bd882a90ca40920d60dde6ff693dd7b5bfc state: error saving container 76bd77687b93a1c15c2c790e920b6bd882a90ca40920d60dde6ff693dd7b5bfc state: container 76bd77687b93a1c15c2c790e920b6bd882a90ca40920d60dde6ff693dd7b5bfc does not exist in DB: no such container 
ERRO[0008] timed out waiting for the condition          
```
How is the container created? With --rm?

Alright we have an interesting race here.

The cleanup process from `--rm` gets in and removes the container while `rm` itself is still sleeping, waiting to check if the process died.

It wakes up and promptly discovers that the container no longer exists.

Of course, I think that our locking ought to be making this impossible, but I have been wrong before.

I'm interpreting this as "@mheon sees the problem", and I can stop looking into reproducers? Because I can't reproduce it with 1.0 nor with root, and was getting ready to spend more time looping on it. May I assume now I don't have to?
@edsantiago Yeah, I'm fairly certain I know what commit introduced this. I'm just puzzled as to how it defeated out locking (which ought to be preventing this scenario)
(Though I have no idea why this reproduces with rootless only... Probably something about fuse-overlay makes the timing work?)
Distraction: I think `podman restart` might be suffering from the same ailment (default-0 timeout). Does it make sense to address it within this PR?

I'll take a look.

@edsantiago I can't reproduce that rootless thing, which makes me sort of convinced it's a race... But one environment question: what's /dev/shm/ look like in your test environment? Any chance files in there are getting wiped between runs of podman?

```
$ df -T /dev/shm
Filesystem     Type  1K-blocks  Used Available Use% Mounted on
tmpfs          tmpfs   8136264 65172   8071092   1% /dev/shm
```
System has been up for weeks. To my knowledge there is no aggressive tmp-cleaner. Is that what you're asking?
Hmm. Damn. This would make a lot of sense if the second one was grabbing a different set of SHM locks.

Whoops: my bad. I was using an old podman (1.0ish) for the `run` invocation. It's the mix of 1.0 and 1.1 (for the stop command) that causes the error; I can't reproduce when consistently using 1.0 or 1.1. Sorry for the confusion.

OK that suddenly makes a lot of sense.

(Maybe we should add a note somewhere that mixing 1.0 and 1.1 is bad because of this - locking just stops working, with unusual results)
(BTW, restart is (theoretically) also fixed with the latest commit - haven't tested much though)
